questions.alt: Question 1. The expression “actions speak louder than words” means that people are often judged more by what they do than what they say. Which sentence from the story best supports this idea? “Little Shaq had been asking for a kitten for as long as he could remember.” A (paragraph 5) B “We lost, but the rec center is having a really cool event next week.” (paragraph 9) “He wasn't good at remembering to take out the trash, and Mom did have Cc to tell him every morning to make his bed” (paragraph 16) “In all the excitement about the pet fair, Little Shaq had forgotten to tell his D P » parents the big news.” (paragraph 18)

Question 2. Which detail from paragraph 7 best supports the idea that Little Shaq is nervous? A The food sits untouched. B The meatballs make a mess. C His mom feels unhappy. D His father catches his eye.
Question 3. What does “glanced” mean as it is used in paragraph 17? A __ looked for something B__ reached over C picked an object up D _ turned away

Question 4. How do the details in paragraphs 18 through 21 support a theme of the story? A ___ They show that pet fairs are big events. B _ They show that hard work can be rewarded. C They show that instructions should be followed. D They show that families should talk to each other.

Question 5. What does paragraph 24 show about Little Shaq? A Heis thankful because his parents are laughing at a joke he told. B__Heis pleased because his brother and sister agree with him. C = Heis excited because his brother and sister are proud of him. D He is happy because his parents have agreed to think about his question.

Question 6. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A Little Shaq will take care of Flopsy. B Barry hasa fish named Louie. C__ Tater makes a mess at dinner. D Malia is ina school play.

Question 13. Which sentence from the passage best shows a cause-and-effect relationship? “Under the roots of an old pine tree, there is scuffling and snuffling” A (paragraph 1) “Porcupette backs down the tree and wanders over to the nearby stream.” B (paragraph 6) “Beavers are the biggest rodents in North America, but porcupines aren't Cc far behind” (paragraph 7) “The mountain lion slinks away, batting at his mouth where sharp quills D » dangle.” (paragraph 11)

Question 14. Why is the picture important to understanding paragraph 2? A __ It compares a baby porcupine’ size to its mother. B__It suggests that porcupines should fear big animals. C __Itincludes where porcupines go to find food at night. D__It shows what a baby porcupine'’s body looks like.

Question 15. Read this sentence from paragraph 12. Soon she is fast asleep, dreaming of more adventures in her prickly porcupine world. What does the phrase “prickly porcupine world” mean as it is used in this sentence? A anangry world B a world full of excitement Cc a scary world D _ aworld full of plants

Question 16. What does the author most likely think about Porcupette? A __ Porcupette needs help from her mother. B ___ Porcupette should be more careful. C__~Porcupette is able to survive on her own. D___ Porcupette is safe with other animals.

Question 17. Which sentence best states a central idea of the passage? A __ Porcupines are awake at night and sleep during the day. B___Porcupines drink their mother’s milk when they are babies. C__~Porcupines use their quills to stay safe from enemies. D__ Porcupines weigh more than baby black bears at birth.

Question 18. 1g How does the author mainly organize the information in the passage? A __ by telling Porcupette’s problem and the solution B by telling, in order, how Porcupette spends her night C__ by comparing Porcupette’s nights to her days D by describing how some other animals affect Porcupette

Question 19. Sometimes different animals can peacefully share the same community. How does the passage support this idea? A __ by describing how Porcupette is not afraid of raccoons at night B _ byexplaining how the owl causes Porcupette to raise her quills C__byshowing how Porcupette senses the odor of a mountain lion D by telling how the mountain lion is unable to attack Porcupette

Question 20. 9 Which quote best supports a central idea of the story? A _ “This field trip to the zoo might be our last!” (paragraph 2) B “ ‘Remember when the new principal came?’ asked Jamie.” (paragraph 15) C_ “‘Thope so; said Jamie. “We have to save the zoo: ” (paragraph 22) “J think the city should use the money to make the library bigger” D (paragraph 25)

Question 21. How does Jamie support her claim that she and her friends can help save the zoo, even if they are not old enough to vote? A __ byraising money B__ by making posters and fliers C __ byspeaking at the town meeting D __ byexplaining what is wrong with the city

Question 22. In paragraph 31, what does “Jamie’s heart sank” mean? A _ Jamie felt pain in her chest. B _ Jamie felt excited she could vote. C Jamie was sad the zoo lost the vote. D _ Jamie was tired of reading the newspaper.

Question 23. Paragraph 32 shows that Jamie's mother believes A __ the campaign to save the zoo was a bad idea B___ the zoo animals will be fine at the preserve C__ the city will make the library bigger D __ the newspaper should only be read by adults

Question 24. How does Jamie change from the beginning of the story to the end of the story? First, she is excited to save the zoo by campaigning. After, she is thankful the animals will go to a wildlife preserve. First, she is disappointed that the zoo might be closing. After, she is sad B that the animals will be so far away. First, she is sad that she cannot vote to save the zoo. After, she is angry at Cc her city for not voting the way she wanted. First, she is happy that her friend wants to help her. After, she is worried D that she did not do enough for the zoo.

Question 26. How does the author use paragraphs 5 and 10 to develop Vikram? A __ by describing how he is more interested in taking pictures than in a shell by describing how he is a good brother who enjoys spending time with B family C __ by explaining why he and his brother argue all the time by explaining the different kinds of activities he enjoys doing while on D vacation

Question 27. Which sentence best supports a theme of the story? “Back at the cottage, Sabeel ran to his room to admire the shell.” A (paragraph 7) “This time, Sabeel tucked the shell under his pillow, where no one could B see it? (paragraph 11) Cc ‘Thave to return a gift” (paragraph 25) D “With the next wave, he was gone.’ (paragraph 26)
Question 28. 2g What claim does Sabeel make in paragraph 9? A _ that Vikram has stolen the shell B that Vikram does not like him C _ that he has lost the shell D _ that he wants to go to the arcade
Question 29. What do the details in paragraph 22 show about the hermit crab? 29 A It needs water. B It senses danger. C It likes the name Sandy. D It wants to live at Sabeel’s house.
Question 30. What does the word “washed” mean as it is used in paragraph 25? 30 A cleaned with soap B felt worn out C became equal to something D carried by flowing water

Question 31. How does knowing the shell is a hermit crab change Dadi’s thoughts on what Sabeel should do? First, Dadi tells Sabeel the shell is a gift from the sea. After, Dadi thinks A Sabeel needs to return the crab to its home. First, Dadi thinks the shell is very beautiful. After, Dadi thinks Sabeel B should buy a new shell at the boardwalk. First, Dadi wants Sabeel to share his shell with his siblings. After, Dadi Cc thinks Sabeel should treasure his gift on his own. First, Dadi likes the pink inside of the shell. After, Dadi thinks Sabeel D should find a shell the same color as the crab.

questions.alt: Question 1. What claim does Mr. Wheeler make in paragraph 1? A The girls can win the game if they have a plan. B__ The girls will easily win the game. C the girls should work harder during practice. D The girls are too tired to play well.

Question 2. Which sentence from the story best supports a theme about believing in yourself? A “The vital thing is not to concede a goal!” (paragraph 4) “Besides, you have to remember, in this game it’s what goes on inside your B head that matters.” (paragraph 6) “ “Mega talent—a rising soccer superstar; she wrote beside Tyra’s name.” Cc (paragraph 15) “Alicia fed the ball back to Tyra, jumped the sprawling defender, and ran D free” (paragraph 21)

Question 3. What does the reader learn about Tyra in paragraphs 6 through 8? 3 A Tyra knows the team needs more practice. B Tyra is a strong leader for her team. C Tyra likes to talk to the team a lot. D Tyra feels angry with her team.

Question 4. __ Read this sentence from paragraph 7 of the story. The whole team turned to her, hanging on every word. The phrase “hanging on every word” shows that the team is A __ Starting to doubt their ability to win the game B___ ready to end the game so they can relax C listening carefully to what Tyra is saying D ___ not understanding what Tyra plans to do
Question 5. Where does the reader first learn about a change that leads to the team’s win? 5 A paragraph 13 B paragraph 17 C paragraph 18 D paragraph 20

Question 6. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? 6 Mr. Wheeler tells the Fernbridge team to not let the other team score a A goal. Fernbridge must make it through the next forty-five minutes to win the B game. C Natalie gets the ball away from the other team and passes it to Tyra. D Tyra makes the decision to shoot the ball and scores a goal for her team.

Question 13. What do the details in paragraphs 1 and 8 reveal about Spider Woman? 13 A She knows what others need. B She knows about many things. C She is an encouraging person. D She is a very careful person.

Question 14. _ Itis said that it is important to be prepared in case of a problem. Which sentence best shows how this idea is shown in the story? “Tell them to catch my brothers and hunt the animals of the forest now, A __ before they return to the Great Spirit in the hard time to come.” (paragraph 4) “The next morning Little Owl woke to hear her mother calling, ‘Little Owl, go fetch some water? ” (paragraph 5) “She slipped the shell into her pocket, picked up the water jar, and walked Cc back to the house? (paragraph 7) “She expected to find broken bits of eggshell; instead, her eyes fell upon a D handful of dark, purple-red kernels of corn” (paragraph 12)

Question 15. How are paragraph 4 and paragraph 10 related? A __ by telling how the first dream causes Little Owl to have another dream B__ by using dialogue to make a point about Little Owl’s people C__ bycomparing how Little Owl feels after each of her dreams D _ by telling how Little Owl can solve a problem by warning her people

Question 16. Which detail from the story best supports the fish’s claim that Little Owl is a good 16 daughter? A Little Owl eats only a little cake and berries before going to bed. B Little Owl gets out of bed when her mother tells her to go get water. C Little Owl weaves a basket and her mother checks the work. D Little Owl goes to the marsh to gather twigs for basket-making.

Question 17. What does the reader learn about Little Owl in paragraph 6? 17 A She forgets what she is doing easily. B She notices the things around her. C She enjoys collecting things for herself. D She dislikes doing chores in the morning.

Question 18. 1g Read this sentence from paragraph 7. Suddenly her dream came rushing back to her, and she lost her grip on the handle of the jar. What does the phrase “rushing back to her” mean as used in the sentence? A __ Little Owl remembers events from her dream. B The dream causes Little Owl to feel upset. C The jar of water is too heavy for Little Owl. D Little Owl must hurry home with the water.

Question 19. How do Little Owl’s actions in the story support a theme of listening to nature? 19 A She follows directions to weave a good basket. B She works hard collecting water for her family. C She gathers things from the marsh to share. D She plans to tell someone about her dreams.
Question 20. What does “out of the question” mean as used in paragraph 1? 20 A a confusing action B an important goal C extremely interesting D completely impossible

Question 21. Which sentence from the article best expresses a claim the author makes? 21 “A lot of good science starts with simply paying attention and thinking A about what you’ve seen.” (paragraph 2) “If you’ve ever watched bits of ash rising with the smoke from a fire, you’ve B seen it too.” (paragraph 3) “And the king of France wouldn’t allow it until one more experiment was C made.” (paragraph 6) “D’Arlandes leaned out, staring down at the crowd as they stared up at D him.” (paragraph 11)

Question 22. Read this sentence from paragraph 9. 22 He ran a science museum and invented an early type of gas mask to protect workers in sewers. What does the word “ran” mean as it is used in the sentence? A moved at a fast speed B was a candidate for a position C was in charge of something D passed quickly in a direction

Question 23. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the article? 23 A Étienne was a responsible and successful student when he was younger. B Joseph experienced failure both in school and at business. C Joseph and Étienne discovered that bags filled with hot air will rise up. D Joseph and Étienne chose to avoid riding in their own balloon.

Question 24. Based on the article, how does making a deal with D’Arlandes help Rozier? 24 A D’Arlandes convinces the king to let them fly in a balloon. B D’Arlandes gathers a large crowd to watch the balloon flight. C D’Arlandes is skilled at using straw to keep a fire going. D D’Arlandes is familiar with the streets and buildings in the city.

Question 26. Read this sentence from paragraph 1. Roofs covered in plants, or “green roofs,’ are sprouting up all over, from schools to city skyscrapers. What does the word “sprouting” suggest? A __ Rooftop gardens are good for all types of buildings. B._ More green rooftops are being built than ever before. C__ Buildings with green roofs can be easily seen. D Plants grow better when they are on a roof.

Question 27. Many people believe you can turn trash into treasure. Which detail from the article 27 best supports this idea? “Architects are finding all sorts of new ways to build buildings that are A easier on the environment.” (paragraph 2) “Builders have found many creative ways to re-use old materials.” B (paragraph 10) “When it rains, the soil and plants soak up rainwater, which means less C water runs off into drains.” (paragraph 12) “City planners like green buildings because they save money.” D (paragraph 13)

Question 28. How is paragraph 4 organized? 28 A by describing in order the steps for making a wall B by stating an idea about thick walls and giving examples C by sharing a reason for thick walls and the effects they have D by comparing and contrasting different kinds of walls

Question 29. Based on the section “Sun Power,” how do big windows make a building better for 29 the environment? A Windows can be covered by shades. B Windows can be made from recycled glass. C Windows are placed where solar panels could be. D Windows allow in light and heat.

Question 30. How does the section “A Better Home for All” support a central idea of the article? 30 A by describing how green roofs on buildings save energy B by explaining how soil helps plants in different weather conditions C by stating that roofs that are dark become very hot from the sun D by sharing that green buildings help city planners save money

Question 31. The diagram supports the information in the article by showing that A __ wind power causes flags to move B__ buildings can be built to help the environment C__aschool bus can be powered by the sun D _ solar panels change sunlight into electricity

questions.alt: Question 1. What is the main way paragraphs 8 through 11 develop the story? 1 A by introducing Edie’s mother B by suggesting a possible conflict C by introducing Edie’s character D by describing the drive to school

Question 2. Which detail from the story best supports the narrator’s claim that she feels different from others 2 in school? “I don’t know what to say to that, so we are silent as we make our way up the stairs . . .” A (paragraph 15) “Like we don’t belong in certain areas just because no one was expecting us.” B (paragraph 15) C “A new kid in school is always exciting around here . . .” (paragraph 16) D “But the looks are curious more than anything else.” (paragraph 16)

Question 3. In paragraphs 12 through 17, how does the setting affect the plot? 3 A by showing why Edie feels confident B by introducing the characters Edie and Alberta C by describing why Alberta is nervous D by demonstrating why Edie and Alberta are friends

Question 4. Which theme is best supported in paragraphs 16 and 17? A Unfamiliar situations are often difficult for people. B Communication between friends is sometimes silent. C Friendship helps people during difficult times. D___ Some people appear confident wherever they are.

Question 5. Read this sentence from paragraph 17. 5 My stomach flutters and I feel those jitters settling in, right on time. Which statement best describes the narrator’s feelings in this sentence? A She is used to feeling uneasy when going to school. B She is concerned about how the students will treat Edie. C She is worried about having to attend a new school. D She is upset that Edie is receiving most of the attention.

Question 6. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? 6 A “Normally I’d ride my bike, but Ms. Whitman offered to drive us . . .” (paragraph 2) B “It sounds like an entire cabinet of dishes just fell . . .” (paragraph 5) C “Edie chews and swallows the last of her doughnut . . .” (paragraph 14) D “How is the new girl more comfortable than I am . . .” (paragraph 17)

Question 7. The narrator mainly helps the reader understand Edie by describing A__ Edie’s response to the students who look at her B_ whya pair of jeans is important to Edie C__Edie’s decision to wear combat boots at school D__ how Edie takes sips of her mother’s coffee

Question 15. In paragraph 8, why does the author refer to spores as being like “mushroom seeds”? 15 A to explain that spores can grow into mushrooms B to emphasize the small size of most spores C to describe how spores are moved by wind D to explain that mushrooms grow underground

Question 16. Read this claim from paragraph 2. ..- Mueller knows that mushrooms are here-today, gone-tomorrow treasures. How do the details in paragraph 9 support this claim? A__ by referring to the time mushrooms spend underground B__ by describing how mushrooms swell with water C_ _ byreferring to some mushrooms that grow through cement D __ by describing what happens after mushrooms release their spores

Question 17. Which detail best represents a central idea of the article? A “But fungi are all around, even when we don't see them.” (paragraph 6) B “‘Asingle strand is too small to see.” (paragraph 7) C “The color will help him identify the mushroom.” (paragraph 8) D “Unlike plant seeds, spores aren't packed with their own food.” (paragraph 10)

Question 18. Read this sentence from paragraph 12. “We wouldn't even be able to walk around the Earth because of all the dead trees.” What purpose does this sentence serve in the article? A _ tohighlight an important role that fungi play B___ to explain why fungi burst out of the ground C___ to describe how fungi spread over large areas D to indicate a typical place where fungi are found

Question 19. In the section titled “How Fungi Function,’ the author best develops the idea that fungi and 19 plants help each other by A __ describing the way fungi filter water in the soil B__ showing how fungi spores help fruit to develop C describing how fungi roots create food for insects D__ showing how fungi and trees share their food

Question 20. As used in paragraph 14, what does the phrase “like a living internet” suggest about hyphae and tree roots? A Hyphae help provide trees with minerals. B Hyphae and trees are connected in a vast system. C __ Trees need hyphae to grow to larger sizes. D __ Trees and hyphae require each other to survive.

Question 21. In the article, what do both the author and the scientist agree is the main importance of fungi? 21 A their method of reproducing by releasing millions of spores B the number of forms they take as well as the chitin in their cell walls C their designation as a life form that is separate from plants and animals D their role as decomposers as well as their partnerships with other life forms

Question 22. Which detail from the article best supports the claim in paragraph 2 that members of Venturing Crew 115 enjoy going to places that are difficult to reach? “After the holidays, the Venturers get to work on their sleds. Those snowmobiles aren't A going to take care of themselves, after all.” (paragraph 8) “The crew conducts training exercises on a snow-covered airfield. It’s nice and flat and B __ wide open—perfect for beginners and those who need to brush up on their skills.” (paragraph 9) “They start by hauling the snowmobiles and camping gear as far as they can into the c wilderness until the road ends.’ (paragraph 10) “The crew spends several weeks taking classes on proper winter clothing and how to stay warm in the cold environment” (paragraph 16)

Question 23. Which statement best describes how the word choice in paragraphs 4 and 5 reflects the tone of the whole article? The explanation that the Venturers go “to the same spot” in paragraph 4 reflects the A comfortable tone of the article. The description of the natural “thick, high brush” in paragraph 4 reflects the peaceful B tone of the article. c The explanation that there is “12 feet of snow” in paragraph 5 reflects the anxious tone of the article. D The description of the Venturers’ wild campsite as a “paradise” in paragraph 5 reflects the positive tone of the article.

Question 24. How are the details in paragraph 12 connected to the details in paragraph 14? A __ byshowing the first and last steps in the process of building the Venturers’ shelter B__ by describing the problem with shelter building and the solution the Venturers found C__ bycontrasting different methods the Venturers have used to build their shelter D _ byexplaining how the Venturers’ process of building a shelter has changed over time

Question 25. Which detail from the article most likely reveals the author’s opinion about the Venturers’ campsite? “When they get to their spot—about 5,000 feet above sea level—they begin setting up A camp,’ (paragraph 11) “As the fire burns, the platform drops deeper and deeper into the snow, at the rate of B about 1 foot per hour? (paragraph 12) Cc “It’s like a miniature coliseum, and it’s the perfect winter shelter—protected from the wind and warmed by the fire” (paragraph 14) “They are usually able to get back to the same spot the very next weekend for another D three nights” ree nights.’ (paragraph 17)

Question 26. Which sentence best states a central idea of the article? A __ Places where it is difficult to camp often make excellent spots for snowmobiling. B Venturing Crew 115 has to work hard to enjoy being at Snowcamp. C Snowmobiling can sometimes be dangerous for people with a lack of training. D Participating in Snowcamp requires special clothing to protect against the cold.

Question 27. Washington State law requires that all people aged 12 through 16 must pass a Snowmobile Safety Course before they drive a snowmobile. How does this idea connect with the details in the article? A __ Itexplains that Snowcamp is attended by relatively few people because of the strict rules. B It shows that the Venturers do the same amount of work as other people to learn snowmobiling. c It supports the idea that people in Washington State take pride in its snowmobiling opportunities. D__ It supports the idea that snowmobiling can be dangerous and requires practice and skill.

Question 29. Paragraphs 1 and 3 contribute to the story by 29 A foreshadowing events that will happen later B providing details that develop the main character C explaining the problem that the characters will solve D describing events that happened at an earlier time

Question 30. The word “modestly” in paragraph 4 suggests that the narrator A does not want to point out her own talents B does not feel that Tyler is being honest C__ gets teased by the others in her group D gets help from her family on her project

Question 31. What does the phrase “going live” mean as it is used in paragraph 8 of the story? 31 A making the content available to all B announcing the project to the school C requesting feedback from others D accessing the new computer system

Question 32. What is the narrator’s opinion about the club’s name? 32 A It should be decided by the leader. B It should be a secret to students. C It should be more exciting. D It should be easier to understand.

Question 33. How are the narrator and Rosie similar? 33 A __ They both get annoyed at the computers the Intranet Club uses. B They are both interested in telling the school about the Intranet Club. C__ They both take their responsibilities in the Intranet Club seriously. D They are both worried that the Intranet Club interferes with their sports.

Question 34. Which sentence best states a theme of the story? 34 A Working together can make people feel closer. B Completing a task can cause a feeling of pride. C Asking for help can be difficult. D Trying something new can be frightening.

Question 35. This story takes place at a time years ago when computers were not common in classrooms and very few students were taught programming skills. How does this information help develop an important idea about the characters in the story? A Students in the Intranet Club are all patient people who work well with others because they have to share the few computers available to them. Students in the Intranet Club are all exceptional because they try an activity that is new, B difficult, and unknown by other students at their school. c Students in the Intranet Club are all very mature because they choose to work with computers knowing that it will help them in their future careers. D Students in the Intranet Club are persuasive people because they are able to convince their schools to try new technology before it is widely accepted.

questions.alt: Question 1. In paragraphs 3 through 5, how does the narrator’s opinion affect the description of the theater? A The narrator believes the theater is confusing and is glad to have a guided tour. B__The narrator believes the theater is boring, so she considers sliding down the banister. C The narrator believes the theater is unwelcoming because children are forbidden. D The narrator believes the theater is grand, so she describes its structures and elegance.

Question 2. As used in paragraph 11, what does the word “awed” suggest about Aunt Sally? 2 A She is distracted by the activity in the theater. B She is amazed by the magnificence of the place. C She is bothered by the patterns on the wallpaper. D She is embarrassed by the comments of the narrator.

Question 3. What do the details in paragraphs 10 through 12 reveal about the narrator? A __ She is from a small town that is less busy than San Francisco. B___ She wishes she had dressed more nicely for the theater. C __ She often visits the city of San Francisco. D She admires all the objects in the theater.

Question 4. What is the meaning of the word “dispatched” as it is used in paragraph 14? 4 A made to leave the theater B ordered to speak quietly C asked to join the performance D told to listen carefully

Question 5. Music can be a powerful force for some people. How do the details in paragraphs 15 through 18 5 support this idea in the story? A by showing the audience’s reaction to Caruso’s appearance B by telling where Caruso stands during the performance C by describing the narrator’s reaction to Caruso’s singing D by highlighting how Caruso walks to the center of the stage

Question 6. Which detail represents a turning point in the direction of the story? A The cashier gives pieces of paper to the narrator, Aunt Sally, and Mr. Steinberg. B__ The theater guide leads the guests to the foot of a wide, fancy staircase. C Mr. Caruso walks onto the stage and begins to sing. D Mr Steinberg leads the narrator and Aunt Sally to their seats.

Question 7. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A The narrator and Aunt Sally attend an opera rehearsal with Mr. Steinberg. B The narrator wonders if Aunt Sally also wants to slide down the banister. C Aunt Sally tells the narrator to think about past guests of the opera house. D_ Aunt Sally and Mr. Steinberg sit on either side of the narrator in the balcony.

Question 15. Read this sentence from paragraph 5. When a baby elephant first arrives at the orphanage nursery, one of the biggest problems it faces is grief. What does the use of the word “grief” in the article show about baby elephants? A They have strong emotions. B They are unable to be cared for by humans. C They have difficulty bonding with caretakers. D___ They must learn to drink from a bottle.

Question 16. Which sentence best supports the author's indication that raising baby elephants is a great responsibility? “They've been rescuing orphan elephants and successfully reintroducing them to the A wild for more than 25 years.’ (paragraph 2) “When a baby elephant first arrives at the orphanage nursery, one of the biggest problems it faces is grief” (paragraph 5) “They spend every hour—waking and sleeping—with the little orphans, forming a Cc lifelong friendship.” (paragraph 5) D “Sometimes they have to teach a baby how to drink from a bottle” (paragraph 6)

Question 17. Read this sentence from paragraph 5. 17 Elephants are highly intelligent, loving family members. Which detail from the article best supports this claim? “. . . healthy elephants can live 70 years or longer, and their development is remarkably A similar to ours.” (paragraph 3) “. . . a baby elephant will stand under its mother and rest its trunk on her body as it B nurses with its mouth.” (paragraph 6) “. . . it’s ready to move from the nursery to Tsavo National Park, where it will eventually C be released into the wild elephant community.” (paragraph 9) “. . . previously released orphans welcome and befriend the newcomers and help teach D them proper elephant behavior.” (paragraph 10)

Question 18. Which sentence from the article best shows that orphaned elephants often form long-term relationships with human keepers? “It has to be taught how to behave and communicate—even how to use its trunk, which A babies sometimes trip over while walking” (paragraph 4) “As they get more comfortable, the babies begin to wind their trunks around the keepers B instead.” (paragraph 6) c “Not only is the mud fun to play in, but the babies learn that a nice coating of gooey mud can cool them off on a hot day’ (paragraph 8) D “Tt never forgets its human family, though, and will often visit, helping to welcome the newest orphans.” (paragraph 11)

Question 19. How does paragraph 11 provide support for a central idea of the article? 19 A by identifying the age that is appropriate for orphan elephants to be released B by describing the way older elephants will welcome new orphans into a herd C by explaining why elephants are returned to the same stockade each night D by showing that baby elephants make strong connections when cared for

Question 20. In the article, what is the main way the author develops the role of the caretakers? 20 by comparing the way elephants are treated in different parts of the world to the way they A are treated in Kenya by providing examples of the activities that orphaned elephants experience while living B at the Wildlife Trust by describing how orphan elephants are moved from the Wildlife Trust to Tsavo National C Park when they are a year old by explaining how the Wildlife Trust started its mission of saving orphaned elephants D several years before

Question 21. According to a well-known science professor, “Empathy is the ability to share and understand the 21 feelings of another.” How does the author develop this idea in the article? A by describing the process that orphaned elephants must go through to find a new herd B by including detailed descriptions of the activities of baby elephants at a national park by describing how caretakers imitate elephant behaviors to help baby elephants C experience love D by including information about the daily physical needs of rescued orphaned elephants

Question 22. How is John Muir first introduced in paragraph 1 of the article? 22 A with a story about his life B with a listing of his accomplishments C with an example that shows the impact of his work D with a comparison between him and other conservationists

Question 23. What is the main way paragraphs 2 and 3 contribute to the organization of the article? 23 A by providing biographical information about Muir B by giving examples of what inspired Muir to study nature C by sharing stories that come from Muir’s writings D by highlighting Muir’s opinions about the importance of nature

Question 24. Read this sentence from paragraph 5. Often, he carried “only a tin cup, a handful of tea, a loaf of bread” and a journal. What can the reader conclude about Muir from this sentence? A He was often poorly prepared for his trips. B _He usually preferred to be by himself. C He chose to live as simply as possible. D He made sacrifices to study nature.

Question 25. In paragraph 6, what is the main effect of the author’s word choice? 25 A to emphasize the number of glaciers found in Yosemite B to indicate the importance of the lectures Muir gave about Yosemite C to highlight the great size of some natural features in Yosemite D to illustrate the popularity of the articles Muir wrote about Yosemite

Question 26. Which detail best expresses a central idea of the article? 26 A “His love of nature and his writings inspired others to take action . . .” (paragraph 1) “. . . he became restless and decided to explore the world and study nature . . .” B (paragraph 3) C “From there, he headed eastward to see the wilderness . . .” (paragraph 4) “. . . newspaper articles gave him a large audience interested in learning . . .” D (paragraph 6)

Question 27. How did Muir’s experience at a carriage factory most affect his life? 27 A It gave him a reason to leave college and explore the world. B It taught him about the hardships associated with certain jobs. C It showed him the importance of choosing to live in different places. D It made him think carefully about how he wanted to spend his time.

Question 29. Read this sentence from paragraph 7. 29 Pretty soon Audie Brayburn was considered an Honorary Swamp Critter. Which important idea does the author develop in this sentence? A Audie has not told many people that he went to the swamp. B Audie seems to belong in his surroundings in the swamp. C Audie becomes interested in other animals in the swamp. D Audie does not remember how to get out of the swamp.
Question 30. The author’s word choice in paragraph 9 affects the story by A __ introducing an anxious tone B__ introducing an excited tone C creating a playful tone D creating an impatient tone

Question 31. In paragraph 11, the phrase “keeping the beautiful bird within earshot” suggests that 31 A Audie needs to hear the woodpecker to locate it B Audie hopes that hunters will leave the woodpecker alone C Audie wants to avoid frightening the woodpecker D Audie thinks that the song of the woodpecker is pleasant

Question 32. How does paragraph 12 relate to paragraph 16? 32 A Paragraph 12 introduces a new setting that is further described in paragraph 16. B Paragraph 12 describes a decision that the main character regrets in paragraph 16. C Paragraph 12 mentions an event that is described in a flashback in paragraph 16. D Paragraph 12 foreshadows an event that begins to occur in paragraph 16.

Question 33. Which sentence best shows the rising action in the story? “From the time he was fifteen until he turned twenty, he worked for a bakery in southeast A Houston, (paragraph 2) “He had never seen so many old trees, including dead trees that were still standing, B perfect trees for woodpecker nests.” (paragraph 5) C “Only one creature on the entire planet made that sound, only one” (paragraph 10) D “Then he waved the photo in the air until it dried, and slipped it into the ammo can” (paragraph 16)

Question 34. Which statement best expresses a theme of the story? A __ Being alone in nature can relieve stress. B__ Trying a new activity is often rewarding. C Certain locations are potentially dangerous. D___ Some people are driven by their interests.

Question 35. How does the author mainly convey Audie’s character in the story? 35 A by allowing the reader to understand the reasons for his tools B by describing his choices and actions to the reader C by helping the reader understand some of his childhood interests D by sharing his thoughts and concerns with the reader

questions.alt: Question 1. How do paragraphs 1 and 2 develop the character of the narrator? A __ They emphasize the narrator's enthusiasm for learning from Grandmother Five Baskets. B They highlight how much the narrator's life has changed since her time with Grandmother Five Baskets. C__ They suggest that the narrator and Grandmother Five Baskets rely on each other. D They reveal how similar the narrator and Grandmother Five Baskets are.

Question 2. What is the importance of paragraph 8 in the story? 2 A It describes the steps of basketmaking. B It reveals the traits that make a good basketmaker. It gives information about the lessons that Grandmother Five Baskets wants the narrator C to learn. D It provides details about the history between Grandmother Five Baskets and the narrator.

Question 3. Which important idea does the author develop in paragraph 11? 3 A Basketmaking can be a useful skill to have. B People choose to learn new things at different ages. C It is a good idea to accept help when it is offered. D Young people should show respect to those who are older.

Question 4. Some people say that the best way to gain a skill is by participating rather than simply hearing 4 about it or watching someone else. Which detail from the story best shows how this idea is represented? “She told me what I should look for, as we walked along the rutted red road toward the A stand of loblolly pine trees . . .” (paragraph 3) “Anna, this is a good time to gather the pine straw, since there was a heavy rain several B nights ago.” (paragraph 4) “Listening to all the talk among my womenfolk from the time I was small made me eager C for my chance to begin . . .” (paragraph 12) “I learned to rinse it well, and dry it carefully on old window screens laid on rocks in the D sun.” (paragraph 13)

Question 5. In paragraph 15, what does the phrase “a little lopsided, but mine” suggest about the narrator? 5 A She is proud of what she has created. B She is concerned about her basket’s appearance. C She understands the lessons Grandmother Five Baskets teaches. D She questions Grandmother Five Baskets’ basketmaking process.

Question 6. Which detail most clearly shows that Grandmother Five Baskets is confident in the narrator’s 6 abilities? “Grandmother Five Baskets moved slowly through the woods, stopping now and again, A each time calling me back to look at one plant or another . . .” (paragraph 6) “Little one, you will find yourself tested now that you have decided to learn these B lessons.” (paragraph 8) “During the next few days, Grandmother Five Baskets showed me how to clean the pine C straw we had collected . . .” (paragraph 13) “Do not judge this first basket too harshly, for your skills will improve with each one you D complete.” (paragraph 16)

Question 7. The author develops the character of Grandmother Five Baskets by showing A __ the steps of her basketmaking process B what she thinks about how the narrator makes a basket C__ howshe guides the narrator D__howshe feels about her role in the community

Question 15. How do the events in the section “A Happy Accident” contribute to the development of a central 15 idea of the article? A by describing what made Perkin want to aim for a higher income B by showing how Perkin began focusing on a new use for his discovery C by telling how Perkin learned about the importance of fashion to the public D by detailing when Perkin realized he was more a businessman than a scientist

Question 16. Read this sentence from paragraph 7. These dyes could vary in shade (so you wouldn't always know what exact color they would turn cloth), and they could fade or rub off. How does this sentence best help the reader understand Perkin’s actions? A __ It shows that Perkin began to notice and follow cultural trends. B__It shows that Perkin saw an opportunity to work with his family. C__ It shows that Perkin identified a problem that needed a solution. D__It shows that Perkin recognized a way to impress the royal class.

Question 17. Which part of the article best reflects the author’s opinion of the topic? 17 A the title “Miraculous Mauve” B the heading “Young Science, Young Scientist” C the details about quinine in paragraph 4 D the information about using natural materials as dyes in paragraph 7

Question 18. As used in paragraph 9, what does the phrase “mauve mania” suggest? A __ People became obsessed with wearing the color. B The dye required an investment of a large sum of money. C_ The dye caused illness because of its chemical content. D__ People became angry that only royalty could wear the color.

Question 19. Prominent or famous people often influence the culture in which they live. How do the details in paragraphs 9 and 10 develop this idea? A __ by describing the details of the royal fashions B___ by mentioning the building of a new factory C__bysuggesting that dye-making quickly became very profitable D _ byillustrating why people from all classes wanted to use the new color

Question 20. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the article? 20 “. . . a teenager named William Henry Perkin found himself captivated by this fascinating A science.” (paragraph 3) “. . . Young Perkin realized that this chemical would make a great dye for fabrics.” B (paragraph 6) “Perkin himself kept inventing, making more new colors, including Britannia Violet and C Perkin’s Green . . .” (paragraph 11) “Today, the Perkin Medal, named in his honor, is presented each year to a scientist in the D United States . . .” (paragraph 14)

Question 21. How does the author develop the discovery of a purple dye in the article? 21 A by telling stories about moments in Perkin’s life B by describing the many benefits of the color mauve C by sharing the series of steps that led to a new color D by naming people who helped Perkin’s efforts along the way
Question 22. As used in paragraph 2, what does the word “irritated” mean? 22 A injured B distracted C annoyed D offended

Question 23. Which idea best expresses a theme of paragraph 2? 23 A Animals react negatively when they encounter humans. B Familiar surroundings can bring calm to living creatures. C Humans have a positive influence when they enter nature. D People can have a negative effect on animal happiness.
Question 24. What is the definition of “good-tempered” as used in paragraph 3? 24 A having a pleasant nature B being organized and ready C experiencing strong emotions D showing anger through actions

Question 25. How do Alice’s actions in paragraphs 12 through 14 affect the resolution of the story? 25 A She shares her opinion about the situation with Carl and Bob. B She works with Carl and Bob to execute a plan for the two colonies. C She allows Carl and Bob the opportunity to learn from the experience. D She ensures that the bees are unable to sting Carl and Bob.

Question 26. Which sentence shows a change in the direction of the story? A “They failed to recognize them? (paragraph 2) “Alice gently blew a little smoke across the entrance, driving in the guards; then she blew B a stronger puff” (paragraph 5) C “Here’s something wrong!” (paragraph 10) “Carl uncovered the weak colony again, while Bob pried off the bottom from the D queenless one.” (paragraph 14)

Question 27. Which detail best supports Alice’s claim in paragraph 16? A __ “Instead of a crowded mass of bees, only a little cluster showed . . ” (paragraph 8) B “The queen must have perished in the winter” (paragraph 11) C “... alittle disturbance as the bees from the two colonies mixed” (paragraph 15) D “Then all was quiet; a contented hum arose within.” (paragraph 15)

Question 29. Which detail from paragraph 1 best develops Chengli’s claim that the journey is long and dull? A “Three weeks on the road” B “Hour after hour, it was all the same” C “..sometimes a farmer could be seen walking behind his ox...” D “The caravan did not at these villages . ..”

Question 30. What effect does the phrase “and now this” in paragraph 3 have on the tone of the story? A __Itcreates a fearful tone. B__It creates a surprised tone. C__ It develops a determined tone. D__It develops a hopeless tone.

Question 31. Which theme is developed in paragraphs 1 through 6? A__Itis difficult to be patient. B Hard work is sometimes a test of endurance. C Nature is beautiful. D People can find joy in bad situations.

Question 32. What do the details in paragraph 9 reveal about Chengli? 32 A He desires better working conditions. B He prefers the company of animals over people. C He cares about the well-being of the animals. D He is eager to impress the people with whom he works.

Question 33. What are the different ways Chengli and Fourth Brother view their roles in the caravan in 33 paragraphs 6 through 10? A Chengli feels ignored, while Fourth Brother feels undervalued. Chengli is assigned to the animals he prefers, while Fourth Brother is assigned to B creatures he dislikes. C Chengli believes he has been rewarded, while Fourth Brother feels he has been punished. D Chengli shows care for the animals, while Fourth Brother shows disregard for them.
Question 34. What character trait is best illustrated by Chengli’s thoughts and actions in paragraphs 9 and 10? 34 A an ability to adapt B shyness C playfulness D a desire for freedom

Question 35. President Barack Obama said in his farewell address in 2017, “If you're walking down the right path, and you're willing to keep walking, eventually you'll make progress.” Which detail best shows how this idea is represented in the story? “He moved back along the line of camels assigned to his care, checking each load until he A came to Fourth Brother, who was checking on his own assigned animals, the donkeys” (paragraph 6) “As the day wore on, however, the rain slackened and finally stopped. Master chose a B resting spot on well-drained land, and the call came down the line to settle for the night” (paragraph 8) “The warmth of early spring made their thick, wooly fur begin to loosen and fall out, but when Chengli first tried to brush it, the camel turned and aimed a mouthful of stinking Cc spit at him. Within the first week, however, hed learned how to keep the animals calm” (paragraph 9) “Uncle Tao often made his way to the end of the line to see that Chengli was learning the D finer points of caring for the camels, quietly teaching the boy whatever he needed to understand” (paragraph 10)

Question 36. Read this sentence from paragraph 3. Areas of the United States with especially colorful fall displays attract thousands of leaf peepers. Which phrase helps the reader to understand the meaning of “leaf peepers” as used in the sentence? A “These tourists flock to the region . ..” (paragraph 1) B “Being in the Northeast during autumn . . ” (paragraph 2) C ‘Lee studies leaf color ..” (paragraph 3) D_ = “The chemical chlorophyll, which gives leaves their green color . .” (paragraph 4)

Question 37. Which statement best expresses the author's belief in paragraph 6? A What people enjoy now will change over time. B__ Scientists should avoid trying to make predictions. C__ There is much for scientists to learn now and in the future. D The behavior of trees is too complex for people to fully comprehend.

Question 38. How does the section “Food factory” contribute to the article’s organization? A __ Itexplains the cause of leaf color change in some plants. B___ It provides a counterargument in the debate as to why leaves change color. C__Itintroduces the idea that color change may be harmful to trees. D __ It presents a problem that scientists are attempting to solve through experimentation.

Question 39. What does the phrase “work like a sunscreen” mean as it is used in paragraph 13? A Certain chemicals cause tree leaves to change color when the seasons change. B The same chemical can be used by people and plants for sun protection. C Leaves are drying up from the heat of the sun when they turn red. D The color red may shield plants from environmental damage.

B The author details current research, then challenges its results. C__ The author introduces new ideas, then summarizes the supporting evidence. D The author recalls old disagreements, then invites the reader to make a judgment.

Question 41. Which claim is most strongly supported with evidence in the article? 41 A People enjoy viewing the changes in leaf color in the fall. B Red leaf color may have different benefits for plants. C Red leaf color discourages insects from eating the leaves. D Humans may affect how leaf color changes in the future.

B__ Eachscientist rejects the other’s ideas about leaf color change. C___ These scientists contribute different ideas to the study of leaf color change. D These scientists want to create new species of plants to study leaf color change.

I said to myself.” (paragraph 21) D “His masked eyes glowed like smoldering coals.” (paragraph 31)

The author wants to prove that Roxie’s method of analysis is better than other kinds of D analysis.

Now, only a few B were left and those were dwindling in size . .” (paragraph 4) “Greta knew every stone, every curve of it for miles up over the high pastures and then c down again toward the sea.” (paragraph 5) “Fog had always seemed to her like the magic spell in the old fairy tales—a spell that D caught you up and kept you as safe...” (paragraph 12)

You’d best go look for her . . .” C (paragraph 7) “It was the most exciting thing that had ever happened to her in her whole life.” D (paragraph 13)

It would be a small D rocket . . .” (paragraph 13)

Seager’s team uses different techniques and scientific methods to study exoplanets and C atmospheres. Seager’s unique thinking and interests have driven her to learn about many different D topics.

Leaping into the air, they barked to each Cc » other. A game!” (paragraph 7) D “Ruined homework! Broken glasses!” (paragraph 10)

Which detail from the story best tells what Grandmother believes? 29 “Grandmother’s big moon tortillas were the best in the world!” A (paragraph 1) “When we have a problem we must choose what we will be.” B (paragraph 16) C “When the eagle is high up, it sees how small the earth is.” (paragraph 19) “Grandmother wound some tape around the arm of the glasses.” D (paragraph 20)

Question 30. What does Marta most likely think about Grandmother’s healing song? 30 A It reminds her of a story. B It helps her make a choice. C It makes her want to dance. D It teaches her about animals.

Question 31. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story? A Marta’s stomach makes loud noises. B The tortillas are in the cookhouse. C The tortillas bubble and turn brown. D Marta decides to be like an eagle.

questions.alt: Question 1. What does the word “muffles” mean in paragraph 3? 1 A changes to a slow speed B moves to a new stage C causes things to get darker D makes things difficult to notice

Question 2. What is a central idea of the section “All Through the Night . . ”? A _ Falling asleep takes less than thirty minutes for most people. B __ Odd feelings while falling asleep are normal. C__ Time passes differently while falling asleep. D Falling asleep is an experience that involves the whole body.

Question 3. Which detail best expresses a claim the author makes about sleep? 3 “Sleep is so useful that all animals do it. And without it, waking life would A be impossible.” (paragraph 2) “As you slip into sleep, your heart slows down and you breathe more B slowly.” (paragraph 4) “During deep sleep, long, slow waves of electrical impulses move through C the brain.” (paragraph 7) “In the next stage of sleep, your muscles go completely slack. Your body D doesn’t move at all.” (paragraph 8)

Question 4. _ Based on the article, how does REM sleep help people? A __ The eyes move back and forth while they are closed. B__ The brain links experiences and memories. C People can begin having strange dreams. D__ People sleep more deeply when it is almost morning.

How does the author organize the information in paragraph 14? 5 A by stating a problem with sleep and then presenting a solution B by sharing a cause-and-effect situation when sleep is interrupted C by explaining the steps to learning a new game D by comparing how long people dream at night

Question 6. Which sentence best describes how the chart adds to the information in the article? 6 A It explains how sleep helps keep the body healthy. B It shares the steps to fall asleep easily. C It offers facts about how the body reacts to sleep. D It compares being awake to being asleep.

Question 19. How does the author organize the information in paragraphs 5 through 10? 19 A by comparing each item that Dustin pulls out of the wallet B by giving the reasons why Dustin must search for the wallet’s owner C by listing the order of events that lead to Dustin finding the wallet D by showing how the clues cause Dustin to find the wallet’s owner

Question 20. 29 What do the details in paragraph 13 reveal about Salvador? A __ His belongings often get lost on the train. B __ He enjoys visiting the city where Dustin lives. C__sHeis grateful for Dustin’s act of kindness. D His family is very important to him.

Question 21. Which statement best explains why Dustin claims, “This has been the best adventure ever!” in paragraph 17? A ___ Dustin's actions lead to an invitation to Rocket Land with Salvador’s family. B Marisa is proud and makes Dustin's favorite cake to celebrate. Dustin has made the day pass quickly by searching for the owner of the C wallet. D__ Salvador’s wallet has many clues to help Dustin solve the mystery.

Question 22. What does the word “approval” suggest as used in paragraph 25? A __ Everyone is ready to leave for Rocket Land. B _ Dustin is allowed to sit with Hugo and Carlos. C The boys may eat the snacks Emerita has baked. D The blue van will take everyone to Rocket Land.

Question 23. Which sentence best states a theme of the story? A the best mysteries are solved using teamwork. B People in different cities can have a lot in common. C Helping others can bring surprising rewards. D Pets are important for bringing families together.

Question 26. How is paragraph 3 organized in the story? 26 A by making comparisons between two characters B by telling the solution to a problem C by giving the answer to a question D by describing events in the order they happen

Question 27. Read this sentence from paragraph 6. 27 I rattled her courtyard fence and called, “Are you there?” What does the word “rattled” mean as used in this sentence? A made someone feel excited B noticed a door or a gate C walked around to wait for someone D made a noise by moving something

Question 28. What do Miss Sabines’ actions in paragraph 8 reveal most about her? 28 A She cares about the narrator. B She thinks the narrator is funny. C She is a very curious person. D She likes when people visit her.

Question 29. Which sentence from the story best describes the setting? “Even so, I enjoyed making the steep but quick (ten-minute) climb to A Miss Sabines’ house.” (paragraph 2) “It has a big garden, with beautiful desert plants and herbs growing everywhere, and a courtyard that has tiles painted bright colors, with B sculptures, pinwheels, wind chimes, and lawn ornaments scattered throughout” (paragraph 5) “The pleasant voice came floating across the courtyard walls, putting me c instantly at ease” (paragraph 7) “Miss Sabines showed me some of her new plantings, we played with her pets, and I leafed through a collection of photographs by Ansel Adams, D whom Miss Sabines calls a ‘poet of Southwestern landscapes: ” (paragraph 11)

Question 30. Sometimes a place can be special to a person. Which detail best shows how this idea is developed in the story? “The sun was turning the Sangre de Cristo Mountains a rosy gold color.” A (paragraph 1) “Speaking of daydreams, I often fantasize about living in a house like B Miss Sabines. ” (paragraph 5) “We passed the rest of the afternoon puttering around the house.” c (paragraph 11) “We're having a party tonight, and I’ve been gone for hours!’ ” D (paragraph 12)

Question 31. Which sentence best states a theme of the story? 31 A It is important for people to not waste their time. B Hard work always pays off in the end. C Friends can help us feel better when we are upset. D It is important to respect your elders.

questions.alt: Question 8. Based on information in the article, what can the reader conclude is the most likely reason Yo-Yo 8 was able to play a public recital when he was only five years old? A Yo-Yo realized that he enjoyed performing for an audience’s reaction. B Yo-Yo was ready to perform because of his father’s teaching. C Yo-Yo had finally found an instrument that he enjoyed playing. D Yo-Yo knew he would perform the music well because of his excellent memory.

Question 9. Which statement best describes the relationship between Yo-Yo Ma and his parents? A __ They support Yo-Yo’s early desire to become a cello player. B They all enjoy listening to different types of music. C__ They hope Yo-Yo’s choice of instrument will be the piano. They are all talented enough to become famous musicians. D Y S

Question 10. Which detail from the article best shows how Yo-Yo connects with people when he performs? “Yo-Yo Ma has been playing the cello since he was a young boy. He gave his first concert A atthe age of five.” (paragraph 1) “Yo-Yo Ma rocks his cello from side to side. He draws the bow across its strings.” B (paragraph 2) c “He throws his head back. His eyes close, his glasses reflecting the bright lights of the stage.” (paragraph 2) “The audience grows quiet. Soon they are drawn into a story he tells using his cello” D (paragraph 3)

Question 11. Which detail from paragraph 6 best supports the meaning of the phrase “as natural as breathing” 11 in the same paragraph? A “surrounded by music” B “trained as an opera singer” C “played piano and violin” D “records of classical music”

Question 12. Why are paragraphs 6 and 7 important to the article? A They suggest that Yo-Yo’s musical talents come naturally to him. B___ They show how Yo-Yos parents greatly influence his passion for music. C __ They explain why Yo-Yo has more interest in the cello than the violin. D They describe how Yo-Yo quickly learns to play because of his teacher.

Question 13. Which central idea is supported by details in paragraphs 12 and 13? 13 A Family members are necessary for success. B Learning something new can prove challenging. C Small problems rarely cause people to feel stress. D Breaking large tasks into smaller parts helps learners.

Question 14. Read this claim from paragraph 15. 14 Dr. Ma said Yo-Yo played “like a shining star.” Which phrase from paragraph 15 best supports this claim? A “had a good memory” B “concentrated better than most” C “could play three Bach suites by heart” D “gave his first public recital at the University of Paris”

Question 22. Which statement best expresses First Woman’s relationship with the First People? 22 A She hopes her people will not become discouraged. B She is focused on bringing new types of light to her people. C She believes her people should be happy with the light they have. D She is frustrated that her efforts are not appreciated by her people.

Question 23. Which phrase best supports the meaning of “illuminate” as used in paragraph 1? A __ “too far away to bring heat” (paragraph 1) B “They would give more light” (paragraph 3) C “They flicker on and off” (paragraph 4) D “the people brought her a slab of quartz” (paragraph 6)

Question 24. How does the narrator mainly develop the character of First Woman? 24 A by describing her actions B by referring to her visit to Fire Mountain C by sharing her thoughts D by showing her working with First Man

Question 25. Which sentence best represents the turning point in the story? 25 “Eventually, First Woman had an idea for a different way to send light to Earth.” A (paragraph 6) “On the rim, they placed bird feathers—cardinal, lark, and eagle—to fly it through the B sky and spread the rays of heat and light in the four directions.” (paragraph 7) “At that moment, two ancient, wise men stepped forward and offered to help.” C (paragraph 11) “Once the Sun and the Moon were following their paths through the sky, the people D returned to where First Woman had carved the wheels.” (paragraph 12)

Question 26. Which detail best expresses a theme of being determined? 26 “So First Woman sent Glowworm, Fox Fire, Lightning Beetle, and Firefly to the four A corners.” (paragraph 3) B “She consulted Fire Man on his glowing mountain.” (paragraph 4) C “It took a lot of hard work and a lot of help . . .” (paragraph 6) “. . . they worked with their flint knives, chisels, and stone hammers to shape the stars.” D (paragraph 13)
Question 29. What is the meaning of the word “bask” in paragraph 1? 29 A to enjoy B to hide C to ignore D to swim

Question 30. Paragraph 10 is important to the article because it 30 A describes why a biologist “loves to look at turtles” B questions why turtles are rarely “all the same shape” C suggests that some turtles have round “dome shells” D states that various shells are better for “different turtles”

Question 31. According to scientists, turtles have existed on this planet for more than 220 million years. How 31 does the author support this idea in the article? A by explaining that turtles belong to “the same family as snakes and lizards” (paragraph 3) B by referring to a “fossil and other clues” that show how shells have changed (paragraph 4) by explaining that it is possible to “see from the ridges how much the turtle has grown” C (paragraph 7) by referring to the “flat shape and leathery shell” that help alligator snapping turtles swim D quickly (paragraph 11)

Question 32. Which phrase from paragraph 14 best supports the meaning of the word “camouflage” in the 32 same paragraph? A “a lighter color on their plastron” B “looking down into the water” C “blends in with the dark water” D “predators swimming below”

Question 33. According to information in the section “What Shape Is Your Shell?”, how do some turtles catch 33 their prey? A by making holes in the sand B by remaining still and using their tongues C by pulling their legs into their shells D by having brown shells and yellow on their bellies

The diagram helps the reader better understand the article by showing A __ the location of different parts of a turtle’s shell B__ the size of the scutes found on a turtle’s shell C__ how the bridges connect the shell to the plastron D how the turtle is able to avoid different predators

Question 35. Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the article? 35 A Turtle muscles attach to shells in the same way human muscles attach to bones. D Scutes protect the turtle’s bones and provide color to the shell.

questions.alt: Question 8. How does the author introduce the idea that the Purple code finally began to be understood? 8 A by indicating that many mathematicians were working on the messages B by explaining how a person noticed something interesting about the symbols C by indicating that the messages were created by a special machine D by explaining how a system was used to obtain signals from other countries

Question 9. What is the purpose for including the information in paragraphs 5 through 7 in the article? 9 A to illustrate the relationship between different countries B to indicate a common naming method for codes C to indicate the importance of understanding secret messages D to illustrate the reasons why a global conflict occurred

Question 10. Which claim by the author is best supported by evidence in the article? A “Many cryptanalysts were people who studied how languages worked” (paragraph 4) “... Grotjan was one of just a few women working at the highest levels of code breaking” B (paragraph 5) “The United States had not entered the war, but relations with Japan were becoming C more and more strained” (paragraph 7) “... the United States used a complex system to intercept the communications and break D » the codes” (paragraph 7)

Question 11. What does the information in paragraphs 8 and 9 mainly illustrate? 11 A how coded messages were created using a special machine B why understanding coded messages was a difficult process C why cryptanalysts made guesses about coded messages D how coded messages were translated into a different language

Question 12. How does paragraph 11 strengthen the author’s central claim? 12 A by explaining the value of decoded messages to the U.S. government B by referring to the places where coded messages originated C by describing how U.S. agencies built a cipher machine D by indicating the name given to messages obtained from cipher machines

Question 13. What is the purpose of the illustration in the article? 13 A to show a device used to read coded messages B to explain how messages were coded C to show an example of a coded message D to explain why some messages were coded

Question 14. Which detail from the article best reveals the importance of Grotjan’s discovery? “Their cries of “That’s it! ‘Whoopee!’ ‘Hooray!’ broke the silence of the room, which was A usually as quiet as a library.’ (paragraph 3) “Each symbol stood for a letter, but the letter it stood for changed as the machine moved B forward.” (paragraph 9) “... the SIS and U.S. Navy built a Purple cipher machine just like the decoding machines c used in Japanese embassies.” (paragraph 11) D “,.. Magic contributed enormously to the defeat of the enemy, greatly shortened the war, and saved many thousands of lives.” (paragraph 12)

Question 22. What important idea do the authors develop in paragraph 3? A___ Classrooms should continue to be updated. B Some modern schools have a connection to the past. C Older buildings often lacked necessary conveniences. D —_ Methods of teaching can change over the years.

Why is this detail important to the article? A __ Ithighlights the general popularity of historical attractions. B__It indicates that students are often interested in the history of education. C__It suggests the importance of preserving historical buildings. D It shows that a connection can be made with students from another time.

Question 24. Traditions allow people to both honor the past and celebrate the present. Which detail from paragraph 6 best represents this idea? A “There are more than 500 varieties of lilacs and more than 1,200 lilac bushes . . ” B “It was beautifully landscaped with trees and shrubs. .” C_ “The park’s collection of lilacs originally started with 20 varieties in 1892” D “Since 1898, Rochester has held a Lilac Festival every May.”

Question 25. In paragraph 9, what is the main way the authors indicate the importance of the Montauk Point 25 Lighthouse? A by referring to its status as the oldest lighthouse in the state B by describing how the lighthouse still provides assistance to ships C by explaining that the lighthouse now uses an automatic beacon D by mentioning the house attached to the lighthouse where families lived

Question 26. How do the authors develop the idea of “legacy” in the article? 26 A by explaining the history that inspired traditions B by providing a chronological sequence of events C by contrasting various celebrations in the state D by referring to the dates when festivals began

Question 29. Read this claim from paragraph 2. 29 Every lichen is a tiny team . . . Which detail from the article best supports this claim? A “lichen on a tree looks like a gray-green, mossy stain” (paragraph 1) B “the loose leaves or decaying logs from which they grew” (paragraph 9) C “the spores of a fungus and another that looked like an alga” (paragraph 10) D “makes food for both itself and the fungus” (paragraph 13)

Question 30. Read the sentence from paragraph 4. “Plenty of people can draw,” he said, “but you . . . have observation.” Which detail from the article best supports this claim? A __ Theauthor highlights Potter’s ability to grow fungi in her kitchen. B The author refers to Potter’s attention to detail in her drawings. C__ The author explains that Potter enjoyed drawing from nature. D__ The author provides evidence that Potter contributed to science.

Question 31. What is the most important way Charles McIntosh influenced Potter’s career? 31 A He provided Potter with different types of fungi to study. B He was familiar with the scientific names of fungi. C He was interested in the fungi paintings Potter made. D He enjoyed collecting fungi while he worked.
Question 32. In paragraph 8, what does the phrase “the fungus starred the ground” mean? A The fungi were bright. B The fungi were tall. C The fungi were hidden. D The fungi were plentiful.

Question 33. What do the details in paragraph 9 most reveal about Beatrix Potter? A __ She was curious about the world. B She was a famous artist. C__ She enjoyed outdoor adventures. D She thought nature should be left alone.

Question 34. Which statement best describes how Potter responded when people doubted her findings? 34 A She argued with botanists until they agreed to listen to her. B She decided to write more papers for scientists about lichens. C She put her drawings of mushrooms on display for others to see. D She chose to spend more of her time working on stories for children.

Question 35. Read the sentence from paragraph 10. 35 Potter also looked at lichens, which scientists grouped with the fungi. Which statement best represents the meaning of this sentence? A Scientists believed lichens and fungi were related. B Scientists had shown little interest in examining lichens. C Scientists wondered if lichens could live separately from algae. D Scientists had discovered algae makes food for itself and its partner.

C_—_He is unconvinced of the safety of the Ferris Wheel. Ds He thinks the attractions will be less crowded later in the week.

Question 23. What does the phrase “on a smaller scale,’ as used in paragraph 2, suggest about spider silk? A __ Spider silk could be used to perform tasks requiring precision. B The simpler uses for spider silk require limited skill. C___ Some uses for spider silk are less important than others. D Spider silk is most useful for large-scale projects.

A the effort it took to collect such a great amount of silk B the length of time it took the spiders to produce the silk C how difficult it was to find the necessary spiders D how important it was to hire dedicated workers

Question 30. Read these sentences from paragraph 5. When they were old enough, when they were permitted to leave the house, they were told not to lose sight of one another. Together they wandered down the winding lanes of the enclave, behind the ponds and across the lowland, to the playing field where they sometimes met up with other boys. What is the main way this change in setting affects Subhash and Udayan? A__ They get into trouble when they wander too far from home. B__They realize they like the quieter areas of their community. C_ They enjoy exploring new territory together. D They prefer being with other children when exploring.

Question 31. In paragraph 8, Subhash and Udayan respond differently to the same situation. What does this show the reader about the characters? A ___ The boys’ parents expect different behavior from them. B__The boys demonstrate different personalities. C__ The boys’ parents give them different responsibilities. D__The boys display different levels of maturity.

Question 32. As used in paragraph 9, what does the word “indistinguishable” show about the brothers? A One brother is considered as intelligent as the other. B They are both tired of playing the game of chess. C One brother acts the same way as the other. D__ They both sound the same when talking.

That was what Udayan did.” (paragraph 11) “Subhash had listened. He had watched through the window, he had not gone out.” D (paragraph 14)

Question 34. What difference between Subhash and Udayan is revealed by the information in paragraph 16? 34 A Udayan pays more attention to detail, while Subhash is unobservant. B Udayan has a more creative personality, while Subhash is carefree. C Subhash dislikes getting dirty, while Udayan likes creating a mess. D Subhash acts as his parents want, while Udayan acts as he wants.

Question 35. Which statement best represents the importance of paragraphs 17 and 18 to the story? The boys’ father understands that the footprints represent an experience in the journey of B growing up. Subhash realizes that the footprints represent a division between himself and Udayan, c which he accepts. D Subhash feels guilty that he did not Udayan from making the footprints in the cement.

Question 37. What does the reader learn about Katherine Dunham from paragraph 5? 37 A She struggles with a career in anthropology. B She blends cultural influences into modern dance. C She wants dance to return to traditional methods. D She leaves dancing in order to become an actor.

B It is a dance that is identified as Dunham’s. C It is a dance that requires Dunham’s style of dress. D It is a dance that includes cultural references.

Question 39. Which sentence best supports a central idea of the article? “They laid the foundation for generations of dancers and choreographers to come.” A (paragraph 2) B “I wanted it to be fraught with inner meaning, with excitement and surge.” (paragraph 3) “Katherine Dunham started dancing in her late teens, but she didn’t think it would turn c into a career” (paragraph 4) D “Primus worked to make African dance an accepted and important art form among American audiences.” (paragraph 8)

Question 40. Which sentence best supports the claim made by the author in paragraph 1? “Graham's techniques are still taught today, and her approach to movement has inspired many young dancers to make their careers on the stage.” (paragraph 3) “Dunham spent 18 months in Jamaica, Martinique, Trinidad, and Haiti, gaining the trust B of her subjects so she could learn and perform their ritual dances.’ (paragraph 4) “She received a scholarship to the New Dance Group, which offered dance lessons to professionals and children.” (paragraph 7) “The all-female group was known for avant-garde performances minimalistic in both D movement and stage design.” (paragraph 9)

Question 41. How are the accomplishments of Martha Graham represented in the article? 41 A Graham expands the changes to dance that Katherine Dunham starts. B Graham influences people’s understanding of dance. C Graham believes dancers should be trained similarly to athletes. D Graham emphasizes the need for dancers to have freedom.

C He has been able to play basketball since he was created. D He is able to teach other robots how to play basketball.

Question 2. Read this sentence from paragraph 4. I couldn't wait to see what he'd do next. How does this sentence develop the narrator's viewpoint? A It shows that the narrator is interested in creating a team of basketball-playing robots. B It shows that the narrator believes SwiftBot is unable to perform at the expected level. C It shows that the narrator is unaware of what SwiftBot is programmed to do. D It shows that the narrator does not know which action SwiftBot will perform.

Question 3. What does the phrase "eyes wide" as used in paragraph 6 reveal about the narrator? A He is impressed by SwiftBot's ability. B He is concerned the ball will hit him. C He is confused by SwiftBot's action. D He is disappointed he is unable to the ball.

Question 4. In paragraph 9, why does the narrator say, “I put a bit of backspin on the ball as it left my fingers, wishing for the hundredth time that I could engineer a way for SwiftBot to do the same”? A __ The narrator would like to give SwiftBot as many human-like abilities as possible. B The narrator regrets the limited effort that originally went into SwiftBot’s design. C The narrator realizes that SwiftBot requires many more hours of programming. D The narrator is questioning SwiftBot’s ability to compete with humans.

Question 5. As used in paragraph 11, what does the word “tracked” suggest about the narrator? 5 A He believes he will win the game. B He is paying close attention. C He has doubts about his basketball skills. D He is clumsy in his movements.

Question 7. Which sentence best supports a theme of machines having advantages over humans? “His silver-metallic body was about my height, roughly five feet ten inches, and A mimicked your basic humanoid design: two arms, two legs, one head, and so on.” (paragraph 1) “He landed easily back onto the floor, the springs in his feet doubling as shock B absorbers.” (paragraph 7) “Leather smacked against a carbon-fiber palm as SwiftBot batted the ball back at me” c (paragraph 10) D ‘“SwiftBot didn’t have to worry about getting dehydrated, but I did” (paragraph 15)

B Animals that are scavengers help to clean our ecosystems. C__ Ravens identify food then learn how to get it. D —_ Corvids can survive among humans all around the world.
